title: Strongly-coupled hybrid lattice-plasmons in layered cuprates
authors: Ke-Jun Xu, Nathan Giles-Donovan, Stefano Agrestini, Jaewon Choi, Mirian Garcia-Fernandez, Kejin Zhou, Junfeng He, Costel R. Rotundu, Young S. Lee, Thomas P. Devereaux, Zhi-Xun Shen, Dung-Hai Lee, Robert J. Birgeneau, Wei-Sheng Lee
abstract: Metallic systems with delocalized valence electrons host collective charge density oscillations known as plasmons. On the other hand, conventional insulators do not have free electrons and the low energy charge degrees of freedom are pinned to the ions. The fate of the collective charge excitations in the intermediate regime is an outstanding question. This problem is especially important for strongly correlated systems such as the layered cuprates, where unconventional superconductivity and other emergent phenomena arise from valence electrons on the border between Mott localization and itinerancy. Using resonant inelastic X-ray scattering, we track this evolution in the prototypical electron-doped cuprate Nd2-xCexCuO4. We find a continuous transformation of the low-energy charge response: from an acoustic plasmon in the metallic regime, to a gapped hybrid mode at intermediate doping, and finally to a nearly dispersionless 139 meV excitation at half filling. Remarkably, the 139 meV excitation has approximately twice the energy of the oxygen breathing phonon responsible for the dispersion kink observed in angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy, and is consistent with a putative 2-phonon excitation observed in Raman spectroscopy. These results establish a unified picture of collective charge excitations across the phase diagram of electron-doped cuprates, showing that such modes persist across the Mott transition via strong coupling to lattice degrees of freedom and revealing a missing link in the charge dynamics of carrier doped Mott insulators.
summary: This work uses RIXS to map the collective charge excitations in electron-doped cuprates across their phase diagram. It reveals a continuous evolution of this excitation, transforming from a plasmon to a hybrid mode, which establishes a unified picture of charge dynamics across the Mott transition. This finding links charge fluctuations directly to lattice vibrations, offering insight into the mechanism behind high-temperature superconductivity.
main_problem: The study addresses the outstanding question of how collective charge excitations evolve across the Mott transition in strongly correlated systems like cuprates.
main_result: The charge excitation undergoes a continuous transformation from an acoustic plasmon in the metallic regime to a gapped hybrid mode, culminating in a nearly dispersionless 139 meV excitation at half filling.
method: The research utilizes Resonant Inelastic X-ray Scattering (RIXS) measurements, complemented by comparisons with ARPES and Raman spectroscopy.
model_or_system: The physical system is electron-doped cuprates, specifically $	ext{Nd}_{2-x}	ext{Ce}_x	ext{CuO}_4$, which are strongly correlated materials exhibiting unconventional superconductivity.
key_observables: Low-energy charge response, plasmon dispersion, gapped hybrid mode energy (139 meV), and comparison to oxygen breathing phonon energies.
important_parameters: Doping level ($x$), energy scale (139 meV), and the transition across the Mott insulating to metallic state.
main_assumptions: The charge excitation is understood as a hybrid mode formed by strong coupling between the plasmon and lattice degrees of freedom ($2\Omega$ excitation).
figures_summary: Figures show RIXS spectra tracking the charge excitation dispersion across doping levels, illustrating the transformation from plasmon to gapped/flat mode, and comparing this to phonon features.
paper_structure: The paper systematically tracks the evolution of charge excitations across the doping phase diagram, contrasting the metallic, intermediate, and half-filling regimes, and establishing a unified picture of charge dynamics.
why_interesting: The concept of a charge excitation mode persisting and transforming across a Mott transition via strong coupling to lattice vibrations provides a rich model for understanding emergent quantum phases in correlated materials, relevant to open quantum systems dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Collective charge excitations in Nd2−xCexCuO4 revealed by RIXS. (a) Color plots of energy-momentum RIXS spectra at different dopings measured with σ-polarized incident photons along the (h, 0, 1) direction. The doping levels, defined by the Ce content, are indicated above each panel. Grey dots indicate the fitted positions of the dispersive low energy charge excitations. In the x = 0 sample, blue dots represent the magnon excitation and the grey dots represent the nearly dispersionless charge excitation. (b) Dispersions of the low energy charge excitation extracted from fits of the RIXS spectra for different dopings. Uncertainties are derived from the one-sigma fit confidence...

FIG. 2. Low energy charge excitation in Nd2CuO4. (a) RIXS energy distribution curves along (h, 0, 1) for Nd2CuO4. Numbers next to each curve indicate the h value in r.l.u. Red markers indicate the charge excitation and blue markers highlight the magnon. Arrow pointing at a low energy single phonon peak around 50 meV. Note that this should not be interpreted as the single-phonon (Ω) mode associated with the 139 meV two-phonon (2Ω) excitation. Black curve at the bottom is the Raman spectroscopy data on Nd2CuO4. (b) RIXS curves at the indicated h value along (h, 0, 1), for σ and π photon polarizations. (c) Dispersion of the magnetic and charge excitations extracted from fits of the RIXS...

FIG. 3. Charge excitation dispersion anomalies in doped NCCO. (a) RIXS energy distribution curves along (h, 0, 1) for Nd1.96Ce0.04CuO4. (b-c) Polarization-resolved RIXS spectra at h = 0.08 for π incident photons (b) and σ incident photons (c). Colored curves represent polarization-resolved spectra. Black curves represent spectra with integrated outgoing polarization. (d) Dispersions of excitations in the x = 0.04 sample (h, 0, 1) direction. The solid red circles, solid red diamonds, and solid red squares represent the charge excitation dispersion obtained using different analysis methods (see main text and Extended data Fig. 3). Solid blue circles represent the magnon dispersion. (e) RIXS...

FIG. 4. (a) Schematic of a minimal, homogeneous effective model that captures—upon spatial averaging—the hybridization between a plasmon mode and a non-dispersive 2Ωlattice excitation (see Methods for details). The plasmon velocity decreases with decreasing doping, reflecting the reduced volume fraction of metallic regions and the emergence of a nearly non-dispersive 2Ωmode associated with the Mott insulating phase. In addition, hybridization between these modes leads to an avoided crossing. Here n denotes the carrier density, g the coupling constant, and 2Ωcorresponds to twice the energy of the oxygen phonon mode responsible for the dispersion kink observed in ARPES.
